content.text: A feature flag registry is the authoritative catalog of every feature flag (also called feature toggle) in a system: what it is named, who owns it, what type it is (release gate, operational control, experiment, or permission), when it expires, and its current state across environments. Governance is the set of policies and automated tooling that enforce how flags are created, reviewed, changed, and retired.

In agentic AI systems, feature flags gate the activation of new model versions, prompt templates, reasoning chains, tool integrations, and autonomous behaviors. Without a registry, an engineering organization quickly loses track of which flags are live, what they do, and whether they are safe to remove — creating silent reliability and cost risks.

Ungoverned feature flags are a leading source of operational incidents. Flags that were intended to be temporary persist for months or years, creating conditional code paths that reviewers cannot reason about, increasing latency from unnecessary flag evaluations, and introducing hidden coupling between unrelated features. For AI agents in particular, a stale flag might keep an outdated model version enabled in production while the team believes the new version is fully rolled out.

A registry with governance enforces a lifecycle: flags must be created with an owner and expiry date, changes must pass an approval gate, and flags that have exceeded their expiry or show no evaluation traffic automatically generate cleanup pull requests. This turns flag management from a manual hygiene task into an automated, auditable process.
Every flag change is logged with who changed it, when, and from which CI/CD system. During an incident, engineers can instantly determine which flags were toggled in the preceding window.
Registry entries link each flag to a team and a Slack channel or on-call rotation. On-call engineers know who to page when a flag behaves unexpectedly.

Expiry-date enforcement plus evaluation-traffic analysis lets a bot identify stale flags and file pull requests to remove the flag definition, the evaluation call sites in application code, and the registry entry — eliminating the need for manual sprint cleanups.

Governance policies can require different approval chains based on flag type: releasing a new model version for all users requires a multi-team sign-off, whereas toggling an internal logging flag can be self-approved.
A searchable registry prevents different teams from creating duplicate flags that control the same behavior, reducing flag proliferation and inconsistent states across a microservices estate.

Requiring registry entries and approvals slows down the initial creation of a flag. Teams moving quickly may bypass the process, especially early in a product's life, unless the tooling makes compliance the path of least resistance.
If the registry is not the single source of truth enforced by CI, teams may manually create flags in the flag management UI and never register them, defeating governance.
Ownership fields become stale when teams reorganize. Registry entries require periodic re-attestation (quarterly is common) to stay accurate.
Fetching flag states from a remote service on every agent invocation adds latency. SDKs mitigate this with local caching, but cache-invalidation delays mean flag changes propagate with a brief lag.

The registry is typically a database (or structured config in a git repo) with one record per flag. Each record holds a unique key, a human-readable name, the flag type, the owning team, target environments, a creation timestamp, and an expiry date. Flags are classified by purpose: release flags gate a new feature until it is fully deployed, operational flags control behavior in response to production conditions, experiment flags route traffic to A/B variants, and permission flags gate access by user segment.

A governance policy layer wraps the registry with rules. At creation time, a CI check confirms the flag name matches naming conventions (e.g., <team>.<area>.<description>), that an expiry date is set, and that the creating user has permission to create flags in the requested environment tier. At change time, the approval workflow fires based on the flag type and environment: production changes to release flags require approval from both the owning team and a release manager. All changes are appended to an immutable audit log.

A cleanup pipeline runs daily: it queries the flag management service for evaluation counts over the last 30 days, compares against the registry's expiry dates, and identifies flags that are past their expiry or have zero evaluations. For each stale flag, it opens a pull request that removes the flag definition, the evaluation call sites in application code, and the registry entry. The PR is assigned to the registered owner.

attrs.alt: Feature Flag Registry and Governance architecture: Flag Registry stores name, owner, expiry, and type; Governance Policy enforces naming, approval workflow, audit logging, and RBAC; Flag Lifecycle tracks created, active, deprecated, and deleted states. Consumers are Agent Runtime, CI/CD Pipeline, Observability, and Cleanup Bot. A Tech Debt Gate blocks new flag creation when stale count is too high.

content.text: The registry is the central hub. Governance policy wraps every write operation. The four consumer systems — Agent Runtime (for real-time evaluation), CI/CD Pipeline (for deployment gating), Observability (for alerting on stale flags), and Cleanup Bot (for automated removal) — all read from the registry and feed signals back into the governance layer.
When the flag count exceeds 20-30 active flags.
Below this threshold, team memory and a shared doc may suffice; above it, a registry is essential for discoverability.
When multiple teams share a flag management service.
Cross-team flags need ownership metadata and approval workflows that ad-hoc naming conventions cannot provide.
When the system is subject to compliance audit requirements.
Auditors require evidence that configuration changes have an approval trail. An immutable audit log in the registry provides this.
When operating AI agents in regulated industries.
Flags that toggle autonomous behavior or model versions need the same change-management rigor as application code deployments.

A YAML file in a dedicated git repo works for small teams; a relational database or a service like LaunchDarkly, Unleash, or Flipt works at scale. The git-based approach gives free version history and pull request approval workflows.
At a minimum: key, name, type, owner_team, owner_email, created_at, expires_at, environments, and description.
Write a linter that validates the flag key format against a regex and blocks merges on violation.
Use CODEOWNERS in git or a custom webhook to require owner-team review for changes to existing flags and release-manager sign-off for production enables.
A daily job queries flag evaluation metrics from your flag SDK's analytics endpoint, compares against registry expiry dates, and files PRs for stale flags using the GitHub API.
Emit a metric event each time an agent evaluates a flag, including the flag key, returned variant, and request context.
Set a stale-flag threshold and block new flag creation.
When the count of stale flags exceeds a threshold, CI blocks creation of new flags until the backlog is cleared.

# flags/registry/agents.yaml
flags:
  - key: agents.summarizer.use_claude_3_7
    name: "Summarizer: Use Claude 3.7"
    type: release
    owner_team: agents-platform
    owner_email: agents-platform@company.com
    created_at: 2025-04-01
    expires_at: 2025-07-01
    environments:
      - staging
      - production
    description: >
      Routes the document summarizer agent to Claude 3.7 Sonnet instead of
      Claude 3.5 Sonnet. Remove after full rollout is confirmed stable.
    linked_issue: AGENTS-4421

  - key: agents.codereviewer.extended_context
    name: "Code Reviewer: Extended 200k context window"
    type: ops
    owner_team: agents-platform
    owner_email: agents-platform@company.com
    created_at: 2025-05-10
    expires_at: 2025-08-10
    environments:
      - production
    description: >
      Enables 200k token context window for the code reviewer agent.
      Disable if token costs exceed $500/day threshold.
    linked_issue: AGENTS-4830

A large platform engineering team maintained 340 feature flags across 12 microservices. After an incident in which an undocumented flag accidentally disabled safety filtering in a customer-facing AI agent for three hours, leadership mandated a registry and governance process.

The team migrated to a git-based registry (YAML files, one per service) enforced by a CI linter. All existing flags were registered with six-month expiry dates. A cleanup bot ran weekly: within the first month it identified 87 flags with no evaluation traffic and automatically filed 87 pull requests. Seventy-nine were merged within two sprints, reducing the flag count by 23% and removing approximately 4,000 lines of dead conditional code.

For AI agent-specific flags — those gating model version promotions or autonomous behavior toggles — the team required a dual sign-off: the owning ML team and the platform SRE on-call. This approval chain is now encoded as a CODEOWNERS rule, so GitHub automatically requests reviews whenever those flag files are modified.
Flags created without an expiry date live forever. Treat a missing expiry as a lint error, not a warning.

If engineers can create flags in the flag management UI without a corresponding registry entry, the registry immediately diverges from reality. Enforce registration as a prerequisite for flag creation through API authorization.
One flag should gate one change. A flag named agents.new_features_v2 that controls 12 different behaviors is impossible to safely roll back incrementally.
Filing stale-flag PRs is not enough. Assign a cleanup SLA: if a stale-flag PR sits unmerged for two weeks, the owning team's manager receives an escalation.
Treating all flags with equal risk.

An experiment flag for a UI color change and a release flag for enabling an autonomous agent's code-write capability have vastly different blast radii. Governance policy must tier approval requirements accordingly.
Use structured three-part keys (<team>.<area>.<description>) and enforce them in CI.
Require expiry dates and re-attestation when extending past the original date.
Store the registry in git so every change has a PR, a diff, and a review history.
Differentiate approval requirements by flag type and environment tier; do not apply the same process to all flags.
Run a cleanup bot weekly and assign open stale-flag PRs to the flag owner with a merge SLA.
Link each flag to the issue or ticket that motivated its creation so future readers have business context.
Track flag count and stale percentage as platform engineering KPIs on a team dashboard.

Flag evaluation in a high-throughput agent system requires local caching with an in-process SDK. Fetching flag state over the network on every agent invocation is not acceptable: for an agent that processes 10,000 requests per minute, even a 5 ms flag evaluation overhead adds 50 seconds of aggregate latency per minute. SDKs such as LaunchDarkly's, Unleash's, and OpenFeature-compliant implementations maintain a local in-memory copy of all flag state, updated via a streaming connection, making evaluation sub-millisecond.

For the registry itself, a git-backed YAML store is adequate up to a few thousand flags. Beyond that, a relational database with indexed queries on owner_team, type, and expires_at is needed. The audit log should be append-only and stored separately from the live registry to avoid write contention.
Built-in registry, audit log, approval workflows, and SDKs for 20+ languages
Git-backed storage option, gRPC API, OpenFeature provider
CNCF project; abstracts flag evaluation so the backend can be swapped without code changes
Feature flags plus remote config; audit log in paid tier
Large teams needing real-time targeting, approval flows, and analytics
Infrastructure config; not suitable for per-user or per-request targeting

Feature flags can themselves become a security attack surface. If an attacker can modify a flag state, they may be able to bypass safety guardrails in an AI agent or enable administrative capabilities. Protect the flag management API with the same rigor as application secrets: require mutual TLS or signed JWTs, enforce RBAC so only authorized services and users can write flag state, and alert on unexpected flag changes (a flag toggled outside a deployment window is a potential indicator of compromise).

For reliability, flag evaluation must never become a hard dependency for agent availability. The SDK's local cache must be seeded at startup from a bundled default-state file so the agent can serve requests even if the flag management service is unreachable. Define the default state for every flag explicitly in the registry entry; for autonomous-behavior flags, the safe default is always off.
A flag registry is the authoritative catalog of all feature flags; without it, flags accumulate as invisible technical debt and operational risk.
Governance enforces creation standards (naming, ownership, expiry) and change controls (approval workflow, audit log) through automation rather than relying on developer discipline.
A cleanup bot that files PRs for stale flags is essential; manual flag cleanup is consistently neglected in fast-moving engineering organizations.
For AI agent systems, flags controlling model versions or autonomous behaviors require elevated approval requirements because their blast radius is larger than a typical UI flag.
Local in-process caching keeps flag evaluation sub-millisecond; the flag service must never become a hard dependency for agent availability.
Flag defaults must be defined explicitly for the case where the flag service is unreachable; for autonomous-behavior flags, the safe default is always disabled.
Treat the flag management API as a security surface: protect it with RBAC and monitor for unexpected flag changes outside of deployment windows.
